frameworks.name: semantic surprise / divided attention
frameworks.one_line: Attentional load shapes subjective semantic surprise that biases creativity judgments.

frameworks.name: dual-task cognitive resource theory
frameworks.one_line: Distractor task competes with evaluation for limited attentional resources.

core_question: Do attentional demands from distractions bias how individuals evaluate the creativity of ideas?
key_assumptions: Distractor tasks divert attentional resources from primary evaluation
Creativity decomposes into novelty and usefulness dimensions
manipulations: Presence vs absence of a concurrent distractor task (Treatment vs Control)
Evaluation of 15 alternative uses of everyday objects
key_findings: Treatment group rated alternative uses as more creative on the novelty dimension
No effect of distraction on the usefulness dimension
EEG (event-related and spectral) and pupillometry confirmed attentional diversion in Treatment
Control group expended significantly more cognitive resources on evaluations
interpretations: Divided attention produces subjective semantic surprise that biases novelty ratings upward
Creativity evaluation is dissociable across novelty vs usefulness under attentional load
